title: Blood on Cashmere
stainChemistry: Blood is hemoglobin protein carrying iron (Fe²⁺ when fresh, Fe³⁺ when oxidized). Cashmere is protein fiber—the most delicate of all: finer than wool, prone to pilling and shrinkage. Hemoglobin bonds to cashmere's keratin through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. Cashmere tolerates zero heat, zero agitation, zero enzymes (Protein would digest the cashmere itself). Cold water is essential for protein removal without setting or damaging fiber.
whyThisWorks: Cold water dissolves hemoglobin without agitation risk. Baby shampoo suspends protein gently without alkalinity that would damage cashmere. No bleach, no peroxide (both weaken delicate protein fiber). Acetic acid aids oxidized stain remnants. The sequence respects cashmere's extreme delicacy while removing protein systematically.

spottingProtocol.agent: Ice water (5°C or colder), blot immediately
spottingProtocol.equipment: White cloth, spray bottle with ice water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work from the back of the fabric (reverse side). Blot hard—let the cloth absorb hemoglobin. Never rub cashmere; even gentle rubbing causes pilling. Flip cloth and continue blotting. Ice water prevents protein setting and begins hemoglobin transfer. Most fresh blood transfers in the first 2 minutes if you work immediately.

spottingProtocol.agent: Baby shampoo solution (1 tbsp per cup cold water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix baby shampoo (like Cetaphil Baby Wash, 2% concentration) with cold water. Sponge solution onto stain gently. Press downward only—never rub or scrub. Baby shampoo is pH-neutral and encapsulates residual hemoglobin without alkalinity that would weaken cashmere. After 2-3 minutes, blot gently. Stain lightens as protein suspends.

Rinse thoroughly in cold water. Residual baby shampoo will interfere with oxidizer treatment if present. Work from back to front. Water must run clear with no bubbles or soap film. Each rinse removes a layer of residue. Cashmere is small and densely packed—thorough rinsing is critical.

For lingering red-brown stain (oxidized hemoglobin), apply diluted 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (1 part vinegar to 2 parts cold water) with dropper. Acid breaks iron-protein bonds. Press gently with cloth. After 1 minute, blot. Do not extend treatment—cashmere weakens with prolonged acid exposure. Color should fade visibly.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Sponge, white cloth, 3% peroxide diluted 1:2 with cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: If brown iron oxide remains, Sponge gently onto stain. Peroxide oxidizes iron pigment. Cashmere is protein—use extreme dilution. After 1-2 minutes, blot completely. Never oversoak cashmere.

Rinse thoroughly. All acid and peroxide must be removed before drying. Cashmere retains moisture—residual chemicals will set in fiber. Work back to front. Squeegee gently with cloth to remove excess water.

spottingProtocol.technique: Lay flat on towel, reshape while damp
spottingProtocol.instruction: Squeeze gently in white towel—never wring or twist cashmere. Lay flat on clean dry towel. Reshape while damp if needed. Never use heat, even low. Cashmere dries slowly and must dry flat. Once completely dry, brush gently with soft brush to restore nap.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Immediately blot with ice water from back. Most hemoglobin transfers in first 2 minutes.
REMAINING: Baby shampoo solution (cold), 2-3 minutes, blot, rinse thoroughly in cold water.
RED-BROWN: Diluted 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (1:2 cold), 1 minute max, blot. Then 1% H₂O₂, 1-2 minutes, blot, rinse thoroughly.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water—shrinks and sets protein irreversibly
NEVER rub or agitate—causes pilling and weakens delicate fiber
NEVER use Protein enzymes—digest cashmere protein
NEVER use bleach (chlorine or oxygen)—weakens cashmere
NEVER use strong alkaline cleaners—damage keratin structure
escalation: If blood stain persists after home treatment (ice water + baby shampoo + gentle oxidizer), stop immediately. Professional dry cleaning with cold NSD or solvent system is required. Cashmere is expensive and overtreatment risks permanent damage.

household.name: Cetaphil Baby Wash
household.note: Gentle, pH-neutral shampoo. 2% solution for cashmere protein removal. No sulfates.

household.name: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted)
household.note: For oxidized hemoglobin. Dilute 1:2 with cold water. 1 minute max.

professional.name: Mulsolite NSD
professional.note: Professional NSD for cashmere protein removal.

customerExplanation: Blood on cashmere requires immediate cold water treatment. Cashmere is delicate—zero heat, zero rubbing, no enzymes. We use baby shampoo for protein, dilute acid for color, and extreme caution throughout. If fresh treatment doesn't clear it completely, professional cleaning is safer than risking damage to expensive fiber.
commonMistakes: Using warm water—sets protein and causes shrinkage in cashmere
Rubbing or scrubbing—cashmere pills easily even with gentle pressure
Assuming enzymes help—protease enzymes digest cashmere's own protein structure
Using bleach—cashmere weakens and yellows under bleach exposure
scienceNote: Cashmere fiber is 14-15 microns in diameter—finer than sheep's wool (20-30 microns). This fineness creates surface area for protein bonding but also makes cashmere prone to pilling and weak to mechanical stress. Hemoglobin's iron core (heme group) is what gives blood its color when oxidized. Cold treatment prevents the protein-cashmere bond from strengthening, which would happen under heat. This is why cold-only treatment is non-negotiable.
callPro: If stain remains after baby shampoo + vinegar + dilute peroxide treatment, or if the cashmere shows signs of pilling, color loss, or texture change, stop and call a professional dry cleaner. Cashmere is expensive and recovery becomes risky after extended home treatment.
solventNote: Professional: Cold soak in Mulsolite (5-10 min), gentle squeeze-out (no wringing). Rinse thoroughly cold water. For oxidized hemoglobin, acetic acid spotter (GAA diluted 1:20, cold, 1 min max). If needed, 1.5% dilute H₂O₂ cold (max 2 min). Absolutely no heat. No reducing bleach. VDS post-treatment optional if needed.
